does: This paper introduces a controlled evaluation framework for assessing whether large language models can faithfully simulate students who hold persistent misconceptions. The core contribution is the misconception-contrastive feedback protocol, which tests whether a simulator maintains a coherent misconception-driven belief state and updates selectively when receiving targeted corrective feedback. The authors propose the Selective Flip Score (SFS) metric, which measures the difference in answer-flipping rates between targeted feedback (addressing the root misconception), misaligned feedback (targeting a different plausible misconception), and generic feedback (only indicating wrongness). Evaluating seven LLMs ranging from 4B to 120B parameters across multiple datasets and prompting strategies, they find that simulators exhibit near-zero SFS, indicating they fail to maintain consistent misconceptions and respond equally to all feedback types rather than updating coherently based on the underlying conceptual error.
connects: This work builds on prior research in two main areas: the use of LLMs as simulated students for educational AI systems and the broader challenge of evaluating whether neural models capture meaningful representations. It responds to limitations in existing simulator evaluation methods that rely primarily on output similarity to real students without examining behavioral consistency during interaction. The paper extends work on misconception modeling in intelligent tutoring systems by bringing these classical educational concepts into the LLM era. It also connects to research on consistency and coherence in language model responses, particularly around whether models maintain stable internal states across conversations. The evaluation framework draws inspiration from adversarial and contrastive evaluation approaches used in robustness testing.
enables: This framework enables future development of more faithful student simulators by establishing measurable criteria for misconception coherence and selective learning. The findings suggest research directions for improving LLMs through targeted training objectives that reward maintaining belief states and learning selectively from relevant feedback. The work enables better design of educational AI systems by highlighting that current simulators cannot reliably model student misconceptions, motivating either architectural changes to support persistent beliefs or hybrid approaches combining LLMs with explicit misconception tracking. The evaluation methodology generalizes beyond education to any application requiring simulators with stable, internally coherent behavioral patterns. Future work could explore whether fine-tuning on misconception-aware dialogue data or architectural modifications like working memory systems can improve misconception faithfulness, supporting more trustworthy educational applications.
